About Me
What I Do
I find patterns in data that hold up under scrutiny — and I'm equally focused on knowing when they don't. Most analytical errors aren't in the math. They're in the question that was asked, the proxy that was trusted too far, or the claim that outran what the evidence could actually support. Every project I build is oriented around that standard: not just producing a finding, but being able to defend exactly why it's true and name precisely where it stops being true. The part I find most satisfying is communicating that — taking something analytically complex and making it legible, bounded, and useful to someone who wasn't in the room when the data was being pulled apart.
Background
I didn't take the traditional route into this field. I took the one that made sense to me — certifications over degrees, projects over theory, questions over answers. Self-taught through Google, Microsoft, and Macquarie University via Coursera, with every project built end-to-end: from environment setup and acquisition through cleaning, EDA, visualization, and stakeholder-ready conclusions. Each step documented, each limitation named, each analytical decision traceable to its rationale. That discipline isn't a stylistic preference — it's what makes the work stand up when someone looks closely at it.
How I Think
The decisions that shape an analysis — what the data can actually support, what constraints carry forward, where the real question lives underneath the one that was originally asked — are the ones that matter most and get made earliest. I take that seriously because the cost of getting them wrong doesn't show up immediately. It accumulates quietly, and by the time it surfaces it's harder to correct. My instinct is to front-load rigor: establish the boundaries of what's defensible before building anything on top of them, so that when a stakeholder pushes on a finding, the answer is already in the work.
Approach
Every project follows the same standard: reproducible pipeline, findings cross-validated before they become claims, and a clear record of what the analysis concluded and what it explicitly refused to. The understanding phase is where the most consequential decisions get made — what the data can actually support, what constraints carry forward, and where the real analytical questions live. Everything downstream is cleaner when that phase is treated as the real work, not just a preliminary step before the real work begins.
Currently
Seeking entry-level Data Analyst roles where rigorous thinking is valued over fast answers.
